## Structure

TCP C→S 0x8482 — character-list / character-management
request. Verified 2026-05-10 against 56 retail observations
spanning all 17/17 captures.

Wire framing is the standard NC2 TCP `[fe][len LE16]` prefix
followed by the body (where `len` excludes the 3-byte framing).

```
[0..1]   84 82                  opcode
[2..5]   char_uid LE32           player's character UID (matches
                                  UDPAlive's char_uid; e.g.
                                  0x00017ebd HANNIBAL/AUGUSTO,
                                  0x00017f1a DRSTONE family,
                                  0x0000a2b0 PLAZA/ODA)
[6]      sub-action              01=query (initial), 06=re-query
                                  (post-resume), 05=create,
                                  07=enter-world (sends char data)
[7..8]   tail_len LE16           bytes after [11..28] preamble
                                  (0x00 for 01/06; 0x09 for 05;
                                  0x51=81 for 07)
[9..10]  field_max LE16          0x001e for 01/06 query; 0x0012=18
                                  (max name buffer) for 05/07
[11..14] install_hash LE32       per-install fingerprint, byte-stable
                                  across queries within a session
                                  (varies per char in retail data)
[15..18] session_nonce LE32      varies per request for query
                                  variants (random nonce); zero for
                                  05/07 create+enter-world variants
[19..22] 1f 00 00 00             CONSTANT
[23..26] account_or_slot LE32    small int — account/slot id (varies
                                  per char: 0x33, 0x37, 0x3b, 0x45,
                                  0xc3, 0xa1, 0xa5, 0xf2, 0x80969800)
[27..28] tail_prefix u16         char-bound prefix (e.g. b8cb,
                                  e0df, 38c9, 28c9, c0e2, 50a0,
                                  58b2, 286900) preceding the
                                  variable tail
[29..]   tail (variable)         empty for 01/06; ASCII name + null
                                  for 05; 81-byte
                                  stat-array+name+subskill-block
                                  for 07
```

Sample retail bytes:

```
fe 1e 00  84 82 bd 7e 01 00  01  00 00 1e 00  b3e4f907  d6ce1158  1f 00 00 00  80 96 98 00  58 b2 5f
                              ^sub                       ^nonce              ^slot
   query (sub=01) 33B total — AUGUSTO, char_uid=0x00017ebd

fe 1e 00  84 82 bd 7e 01 00  06  00 00 1e 00  b3e4f907  88ad055a  1f 00 00 00  80 96 98 00  58 b2 72
                              ^sub                       ^nonce              ^slot
   re-query (sub=06) 33B total — AUGUSTO

fe 27 00  84 82 1a 7f 01 00  05  09 00 12 00  0b5ca008  00000000  1f 00 00 00  f2 00 00 00  b8 cb
          45 6c 43 68 61 63 61 6c 00  65
   create (sub=05) 42B total — char name "ElChacal\0" (9 bytes + 1 trailing)

fe 6e 00  84 82 1a 7f 01 00  07  51 00 12 00  0b5ca008  00000000  1f 00 00 00  f2 00 00 00  b8 cb
          [11×LE32 stat array][LE16 09][u8 0c]  "Elchacal\0"  [12×u8-pair subskill block]
   enter-world (sub=07) 113B total — sends back saved char data

```

## Variants

Four sub-actions identified, each with a fixed inner layout
keyed by byte [6]:

| sub | name              | total | tail content                             |
|-----|-------------------|-------|------------------------------------------|
|0x01 | query             | 33B   | none — empty tail, prefix only           |
|0x06 | re-query          | 33B   | none — same shape as 01, fresh nonce     |
|0x05 | create            | 42B   | ASCII char name + null + 1 trailing byte |
|0x07 | enter-world       | 113B  | 11×LE32 stats + ASCII name + 12×u8-pair  |

All variants share the same 28-byte preamble. The sub-action
byte at [6] disambiguates parsing of the tail.

The 0x07 variant's 11×LE32 stat array contains progressive
values (3, 10, 0x0002bc00, 0x00032000, 0x00038400, 0x0003ec00,
0x00044f00, 0x00096100, 0x0010c400, 0x0009c800, 0x00000f00) —
likely XP curve thresholds or skill cap progression. The
12×u8-pair block looks like (subskill_id, rank) pairs for the
character's subskill ranks (e.g. (0x03, 0x14), (0x16, 0x0e),
(0x06, 0x06), (0x0b, 0x28), …).

## Observed contexts

- **0x01 query**: emitted right after Auth — the client
  requests its character list. Always paired with a S→C
  CharList (0x8385) response. 17/17 captures see this.
- **0x06 re-query**: emitted within ~3-5s of the 0x01 query —
  appears to be a follow-up sync probe (different nonce, same
  install hash). Paired with another S→C 0x8385.
- **0x05 create**: emitted only in CREATION_LEVELING_LONG when
  the player creates a new character. Carries the chosen ASCII
  name as null-terminated string.
- **0x07 enter-world**: emitted once per session right before
  the gameserver UDP handshake — the client sends the stat
  array + subskill ranks for its selected character. Possibly
  a "load-character-into-world" RPC.

## Open questions

- The 11×LE32 stat array in 0x07 — these look like XP
  thresholds but exact mapping (which level/curve) not yet
  pinned. Could also be the saved character's accumulated XP
  per-skill-class values.
- The 12×u8-pair block at the end of 0x07 — pair count = 12
  matches NC2's 12 main subskill categories (HLT, ATL, END,
  PSU, etc.). Pair[0]=(0x03,0x14) suggests
  (subskill_id, rank). Need to cross-check against CharInfo S4.
- `field_max LE16` at [9..10] = 18 for create/enter-world
  (matches NC2's 18-byte max-name buffer) — for query variants
  this is 0x1e (=30, possibly the response slot count?).
- `account_or_slot LE32` at [23..26]: byte-stable per char
  across captures (chars sharing a name share this value),
  suggesting it's a character-identifier field rather than
  random.
- `tail_prefix u16` at [27..28]: byte-stable per char
  (HANNIBAL/AUGUSTO=0x58b2, DRSTONE=0xe0df, PLAZA=0x38c9 etc.).
  Possibly a checksum or ServerID nibble.

## Server-side handler

`server.gameserver.packets.client_tcp.GetCharList` — currently
only parses sub-action 0x01 (and lumps 0x06 with the same
handling). Sub-actions 0x05 and 0x07 are NOT specifically
handled — Ceres-J's char-creation flow uses a different RPC
path entirely and char-enter-world data is reconstructed from
DB rather than wire-derived.

The bytes [11..14] install_hash and [15..18] nonce are not
validated server-side; harmless because the response 0x8385
content is built from authenticated session state.

For full retail parity the handler should:
1. Recognise sub-action 0x06 distinctly from 0x01 (same
   response, but log/metrics could distinguish).
2. Parse sub-action 0x05 ASCII name into the create-char path
   (current path receives the name via a separate TCP call).
3. Decode sub-action 0x07's stat array + subskill pairs and
   either validate against DB-stored values or use as
   client-side hint for char-load.
